author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.

ccel.title: NPNF1-07. St. Augustine: Homilies on the Gospel of John; Homilies on the First Epistle of John; Soliloquies

source.title: NPNF1-07. St. Augustine: Homilies on the Gospel of John; Homilies on the First Epistle of John; Soliloquies

title: Chapter XVIII. 13–27.

source.title: NPNF1-07. St. Augustine: Homilies on the Gospel of John; Homilies on the First Epistle of John; Soliloquies

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: After that His persecutors had, through the treason of Judas, taken and bound the Lord, who loved us, and gave Himself for us, and whom the Father spared not, but gave Him up for us all: that we may understand that there was no praise due to Judas for the usefulness of his treachery, but damnation for the willfulness of his wickedness: “They led Him,” as John the evangelist tells us, “to Annas first.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Nor does he withhold the reason for so doing: “For he was father-in-law to Caiaphas, who was the high priest that same year.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Now Caiaphas was he,” he says, “who gave counsel to the Jews, that it was expedient that one man should die for the people.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And properly enough Matthew, when wishing to say the same in fewer words, tells us that He was led to Caiaphas; for He was also taken in the first place to Annas, simply because he was his father-in-law; and where we have only to understand that such was the very thing that Caiaphas wished to be done.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “But Jesus was followed,” he says, “by Simon Peter, and another disciple.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Who that other disciple is, we cannot affirm with confidence, because it is left unnoticed here.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But it is in this way that John usually refers to himself, with the addition, “whom Jesus loved.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Perhaps, therefore, it is he also in the present case; but whoever it is, let us look at what follows.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “And that disciple,” he says, “was known unto the high priest, and went in with Jesus into the palace of the high priest; but Peter stood at the door without.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Then went out that other disciple, who was known unto the high priest, and spake unto her that kept the door, and brought in Peter.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Then saith the damsel that kept the door unto Peter, Art thou also one of this man’s disciples?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: He saith, I am not.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Lo, the pillar of greatest strength has at a single breath of air trembled to its foundations.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Where is now all that boldness of the promiser, and his overweening confidence in himself beforehand?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: What now of those words, when he said, “Why cannot I follow Thee now?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: I will lay down my life for Thy sake.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Is this the way to follow the Master, to deny his own discipleship? is it thus that one’s life is laid down for the Lord, when one is frightened at a maid-servant’s voice, lest it should compel us to the sacrifice?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But what wonder, if God foretold what was true, and man presumptuously imagined what was false?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Assuredly in this denial of the Apostle Peter, which had now entered on its first stage, we ought to take notice that not only is Christ denied by one who says that He is not Christ, but by him also who, while really a Christian, himself denies that he is so.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For the Lord said not to Peter, Thou shalt deny that thou art my disciple; but, “Thou shalt deny me.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Him, therefore, he denied, when he denied that he was His disciple.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And what else did such a form of denial imply, but that of his own Christianity?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For although the disciples of Christ were not yet called by such a name,—because it was after His ascension, in Antioch, first that the disciples began to be called Christians,—yet the thing itself, that afterwards assumed such a name, already existed, those who were afterwards called Christians were already disciples; and this common name, like the common faith, they transmitted to their posterity.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: He, therefore, who denied that he was Christ’s disciple, denied the reality of the thing, of which the being called a Christian was only the name.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: How many afterwards, not to speak of old men and women, whose satiated feelings as regards the present life might more easily enable them to brave death for the confession of Christ; and not merely the youth of both sexes, when of an age at which the exercise of fortitude seems to be fairly required; but even boys and girls could do—even as an innumerable company of holy martyrs with brave hearts and by a violent death entered the kingdom of heaven—what at that moment he was unable to do, who received the keys of that kingdom.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: It is here we see why it was said, “Let these go their way,” when He, who hath redeemed us by His own blood, gave Himself for us; that the saying which He spake might be fulfilled, “Of those whom Thou hast given me I have lost none.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For assuredly, had Peter gone hence after denying the Christ, what else would have awaited him but destruction?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “And the servants and officers stood beside the fire of burning coals, for it was cold, and warmed themselves.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Though it was not winter, it was cold: which is sometimes wont to be the case even at the vernal equinox.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “And Peter was standing with them, and warming himself.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: The high priest then asked Jesus of His disciples, and of His doctrine.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Jesus answered him, I spake openly to the world; I always taught in the synagogue, and in the temple, whither all the Jews resort, and in secret have I said nothing.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Why askest thou me? ask those who heard me, what I have said unto them: behold, they know what I said.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: A question occurs that ought not to be passed over, how it is that the Lord Jesus said, “I spake openly to the world;” and in particular that which He afterwards added, “In secret have I said nothing.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Did He not, even in that latest discourse which He delivered to the disciples after supper, say to them, “These things have I spoken unto you in proverbs; but the hour cometh, when I shall no more speak unto you in proverbs, but I shall show you plainly of my Father?”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: If, then, He spake not openly even to the more intimate company of His disciples, but gave the promise of a time when He would speak openly, how was it that He spake openly to the world?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And still further, as is also testified on the authority of the other evangelists, to those who were truly His own, in comparison with others who were not His disciples, He certainly spake with much greater plainness when He was alone with them at a distance from the multitudes; for then He unfolded to them the parables, which He had uttered in obscure terms to others.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: What then is the meaning of the words, “In secret have I said nothing”?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: It is in this way we are to understand His saying, “I spake openly to the world;” as if He had said, There were many that heard me.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And that word “openly” was in a certain sense openly and in another sense not openly.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: It was openly, because many heard Him; and again it was not openly, because they did not understand Him.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And even what He spake to His disciples apart, He certainly spake not in secret.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For who speaketh in secret, that speaketh before so many persons; as it is written, “At the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established:” especially if that be spoken to a few which he wisheth to become known to many through them; as the Lord Himself said to the few whom He had as yet, “What I tell you in darkness, that speak ye in light; and what ye hear in the ear, that preach ye upon the house-tops”?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And accordingly the very thing that seemed to be spoken by Himself in secret, was in a certain sense not spoken in secret; for it was not so spoken to remain unuttered by those to whom it was spoken; but rather so in order to be preached in every possible direction.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: A thing therefore may be uttered at once openly, and not openly; or at the same time in secret, and yet not in secret, as it is said, “That seeing, they may see, and not see.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For how “may they see,” save only because it is openly, and not in secret; and again, how is it that the same parties “may not see,” save that it is not openly, but in secret?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Howbeit the very things which they had heard without understanding, were such as could not with justice or truth be turned into a criminal charge against Him: and as often as they tried by their questions to find something whereof to accuse Him, He gave them such replies as utterly discomfited all their plots, and left no ground for the calumnies they devised.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Therefore He said, “Why askest thou me? ask those who heard me, what I have said unto them: behold, they know what I said.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “And when He had thus spoken, one of the officers who stood by gave Jesus a blow with his open hand, saying, Answerest thou the high priest so?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Jesus answered him, If I have spoken evil, bear witness of the evil; but if well, why smitest thou me?”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: What could be truer, meeker, juster, than such an answer?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For it is His [reply], from whom the prophetic voice had issued before, “Make for thy goal (literally, take aim), and advance prosperously and reign, because of truth, and meekness, and righteousness.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: If we con sider who it was that received the blow, might we not well feel the wish that he who struck it were either consumed by fire from heaven, or swallowed up by the gaping earth, or seized and carried off by devils, or visited with some other or still heavier punishment of this kind?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For what one of all these could not He, who made the world, have commanded by His power, had He not wished rather to teach us the patience that overcometh the world?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Some one will say here, Why did He not do what He Himself commanded? for to one that smote Him, He ought not to have answered thus, but to have turned to him the other cheek.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Nay, more than this, did He not answer truthfully, and meekly, and righteously, and at the same time not only prepare His other cheek to him who was yet again to smite it, but His whole body to be nailed to the tree?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And hereby He rather showed, what needed to be shown, namely, that those great precepts of His are to be fulfilled not by bodily ostentation, but by the preparation of the heart.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For it is possible that even an angry man may visibly hold out his other cheek.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: How much better, then, is it for one who is inwardly pacified to make a truthful answer, and with tranquil mind hold himself ready for the endurance of heavier sufferings to come?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Happy is he who, in all that he suffers unjustly for righteousness’ sake, can say with truth, “My heart is ready, O God, my heart is ready;” for this it is that gives cause for that which follows: “I will sing and I give praise;” which Paul and Barnabas could do even in the cruellest of bonds.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But let us return to what follows in the Gospel narrative.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “And Annas sent Him bound unto Caiaphas the high priest.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: To him, according to Matthew’s account, He was led at the outset, because he was the high priest that year.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For both the pontiffs are to be understood as in the habit of acting year by year alternately, that is, as chief priests; and these were at that time Annas and Caiaphas, as recorded by the evangelist Luke, when telling of the time when John, the Lord’s forerunner, began to preach the kingdom of heaven and to gather disciples.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For he speaks thus: “Under the high priests Annas and Caiaphas, the word of the Lord came upon John, the son of Zacharias, in the wilderness,” etc. Accordingly these two pontiffs fulfilled their years in turn: and it was the year of Caiaphas when Christ suffered.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And so, according to Matthew, when He was apprehended, He was taken to him; but first, according to John, they came with Him to Annas; not because he was his colleague, but his father-in-law.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And we must suppose that it was by Caiaphas’ wish that it was so done; or that their houses were so situated, that Annas could not properly be overlooked by them as they passed on their way.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But the evangelist, after saying that Annas sent Him bound unto Caiaphas, returns to the place of his narrative, where he had left Peter, in order to explain what had taken place in Annas’ house in regard to his threefold denial.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “But Peter was standing,” he says, “and warming himself.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: He thus repeats what he had already stated before; and then adds what follows.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “They said therefore unto him, Art thou also one of his disciples?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: He denied, and said, I am not.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: He had already denied once; this is the second time.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And then, that the third denial might also be fulfilled, “one of the servants of the high priest, being his kinsman whose ear Peter cut off, saith, Did I not see thee in the garden with him?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Peter then denied again, and immediately the cock crew.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Behold, the prediction of the Physician is fulfilled, the presumption of the sick man is brought to the light.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For there is no performance of what the latter had asserted, “I will lay down my life for Thy sake;” but a performance of what the former had predicted, “Thou shalt thrice deny me.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But with the completion of Peter’s threefold denial, let the present discourse be also now completed, that hereafter we may make a fresh start with the consideration of what was done respecting the Lord before Pontius Pilate the governor.
